title: Instructional tutorial for using demuxlet
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"3ij7q","text":"Genetic multiplexing of barcoded single cell RNA-seq","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":52}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"f5dnk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b5du1","text":"Introduction","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4od3k","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d786q","text":"demuxlet is a software tool to deconvolute sample identity and identify multiplets when multiple samples are pooled by barcoded single cell sequencing.demuxlet takes (1) a SAM/BAM/CRAM file produced by the standard 10x sequencing platform, or any other barcoded single cell RNA-seq (with proper --tag-UMI and --tag-group) options (2) a VCF/BCF file containing the genotype (GT), posterior probability (GP), or genotype likelihood (GL) to assign each barcode to a specific sample (or a pair of samples) in the VCF file.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9},{"style":"bold","offset":151,"length":9},{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":9},{"style":"italic","offset":151,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9kfim","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"sh4d","text":"Tutorial for demuxlet","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ab987","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2hhej","text":"This tutorial provides streamlined instructions for using the tool demuxlet. For a more detailed description of all of the options available to use with demuxlet, please refer to the README (attached in .zip file). demuxlet has several dependencies and this tutorial provides steps to use a docker instance to minimize compatibility issues and an alternative of installing demuxlet from source. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":67,"length":8},{"style":"bold","offset":153,"length":8},{"style":"bold","offset":215,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5vpfk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"f20if","text":"demuxlet is a software tool to deconvolute sample identity and identify multiplets when multiple samples are pooled by barcoded single cell sequencing. demuxlet requires the following input files:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9},{"style":"bold","offset":152,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9bnsr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"invh","text":"a SAM/BAM/CRAM file produced by the standard 10x sequencing platform, or any other barcoded single cell RNA-seq (with proper --tag-UMI and --tag-group) options.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":125,"length":10},{"style":"bold","offset":139,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ei4en","text":"a VCF/BCF file containing the genotype (GT), posterior probability (GP), or genotype likelihood (GL) to assign each barcode to a specific sample (or a pair of samples) in the VCF file.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}

about using demuxlet or suggestions for future releases, please contact of 293T and Jurkat VCF file.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":35}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b7am","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"20s60","text":"293T VCF Source website: http://hek293genome.org/v2/data.php Source file:http://bioinformatics.psb.ugent.be/downloads/genomeview/hek293/SNP/293T_RTG.vcf.gz","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":25,"length":35},{"key":3,"offset":73,"length":82}],"data":[]},{"key":"9t6or","text":"Jurkat VCF Source website: https://zenodo.org/record/400615#.WYIh7IQrLIV Source file: https://zenodo.org/record/400615/files/jurkat_final_variant_calls.tar.gz","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":4,"offset":27,"length":45},{"key":5,"offset":86,"length":72}],"data":[]},{"key":"ekkhc","text":"293T:jurkat VCF file generation We used the CrossMap tool to liftover the 293T vcf file from hg18 to hg19. The tetraploid genotype for the Jurkat vcf was collapsed to a diploid genotype before being merged with the 293T vcf file and the resulting file was filtered to contain only the exon positions.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"aeg8b","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dvpio","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"440am","text":"Installing demuxlet","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":19}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"taoc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2l68h","text":"1. MAC NOTES","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9sm0e","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fr8fg","text":"You will need to install some additional packages on a Mac.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3s9n9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2gkc2","text":"First, you will need XCode CommandLineTools","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"37hcd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6gu5c","text":"Second, it's good to install homebrew:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"12q9e","text":"/usr/bin/ruby -e "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/master/install)" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":6,"offset":0,"length":99}],"data":[]},{"key":"8qlgr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8hn8e","text":"Then, we need to install autoconf, automake, and libtool","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fo8nl","text":"$ brew install autoconf automake libtool ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":7,"offset":0,"length":41}],"data":[]},{"key":"7vmeg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6as7p","text":"We will also need a new clang since Apple's clang doesn't support OpenMPI","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1hbqk","text":"$ brew install llvm $ export CC=/usr/local/Cellar/llvm/4.0.0_1/bin/clang $ export CXX=/usr/local/Cellar/llvm/4.0.0_1/bin/clang++  $ export LDFLAGS=-L/usr/local/Cellar/llvm/4.0.0_1/lib/ ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":8,"offset":0,"length":185}],"data":[]},{"key":"1tqgd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6rsqi","text":"The CRAM format may use LZMA2 compression, which is implemented in HTSlib by using compression routines from liblzma http://tukaani.org/xz/.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":9,"offset":117,"length":22}],"data":[]},{"key":"cisbc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fhll1","text":"Building HTSlib requires liblzma development files to be installed on the build machine; you may need to ensure a package such as liblzma-dev (on Debian or Ubuntu Linux), xz-devel (on RPM-based Linux distributions or Cygwin), or xz (via Homebrew on macOS) is installed; or build XZ Utils from source. Assuming we are on a Mac:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"123mn","text":"$ brew install xz ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":10,"offset":0,"length":18}],"data":[]},{"key":"sipo","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9qld4","text":"2. Install htslib","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2u945","text":"$ git clone https://github.com/samtools/htslib.git $ cd path/to/htslib $ autoheader $ autoconf $ ./configure # optional, --prefix=/path/file $ make $ make install # optional, DESTDIR=/path/file","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":11,"offset":0,"length":193}],"data":[]},{"key":"6uub7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"352oj","text":"3. Install demuxlet","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":19}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2tij7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1c7r6","text":"demuxlet and htslib should be installed in same directory","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ffeld","text":"$ git clone https://github.com/statgen/demuxlet.git $ cd /path/to/demuxlet $ mkdir m4 $ autoreconf -vfi             $ ./configure # optional, --prefix=/path/file $ make $ make install # optional, DESTDIR=/path/file","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":12,"offset":0,"length":214}],"data":[]},{"key":"8eq76","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"82ohb","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4ipcs","text":"Tips for running","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":16}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4d4tk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6kt6o","text":"Set --alpha 0 --alpha 0.5, which assumes the expected proportion of 50% genetic mixture from two individuals, to get better estimates of doublets.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"frnvg","text":"Set --group-list to a list of barcodes (i.e. barcodes.tsv from 10X) to speed things up and only get demultiplexing for cells called by other methods","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cshtg","text":"To reproduce the results presented in Figure 2 of the demuxlet paper, please go to: https://github.com/yelabucsf/demuxlet_paper_code/tree/master/fig2 to download the vcf and the outputs of demuxlet.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":13,"offset":84,"length":65}],"data":[]},{"key":"dibkv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5fao","text":"Using demuxlet","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"emi0q","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"390um","text":"demuxlet uses a self-documentation utility. You can run each utility with -man or -help option to see the command line usages.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"kida","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":14,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"enu9","text":"The detailed usage is also pasted below.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1ec5s","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":15,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"ttt7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"pdng","text":"Interpretation of output files","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":30}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a53hi","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"415vp","text":"demuxlet generates multiple output file, such as [prefix].best, [prefix].sing, [prefix].sing2, and optionally [prefix].pair (with --write-pair argument). Each file contains the following information","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8},{"style":"bold","offset":49,"length":13},{"style":"bold","offset":64,"length":13},{"style":"bold","offset":79,"length":14},{"style":"bold","offset":110,"length":14},{"style":"bold","offset":130,"length":12},{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a4tfi","text":"The [prefix].best file contains the best guess of the sample identity, with detailed statistics to reach to the best guess","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"15mo8","text":"The [prefix].sing file contains the statistics for matching each cell with each possible sample.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2ovs8","text":"The [prefix].sing2 file contains the statistics similar information to the previous one, but generated for sanity checking of the [prefix].pair results.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":14},{"style":"bold","offset":130,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4gns8","text":"The [prefix].pair file contains the statistics for matching each cell with each possible configuration of doublet.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"evkj4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6mgd3","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"aaq9q","text":"The [prefix].best file contains the following 22 columns.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"duhnm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7iafj","text":"1.     BARCODE - Cell barcode for the cell that is being assigned in this row","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8dv4d","text":"2.     RD.TOTL - The total number of reads overlapping with variant sites for each droplet.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3j7km","text":"3.     RD.PASS - The total number of reads that passed the quality threshold, such as mapping quality, base quality.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5ug5r","text":"4.     RD.UNIQ - The total number of UMIs that passed the quality threshold. If a UMI is observed in a single variant         multiple times, it won't be counted more. If a UMI is observed across multiple variants, it will be counted as different.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"73mjg","text":"5.     N.SNP - The total number of variants overlapping with any read in the droplet.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cb2f3","text":"6.     BEST - The best assignment for sample ID.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"duuca","text":"For singlets, SNG-","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dueho","text":"For doublets, DBL---","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4re22","text":"For ambiguous droplets, , AMB---<doublet ID1/ID2>)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2ji1c","text":"7.     SNG.1ST - The best singlet assignment for sample ID","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1psbf","text":"8.     SNG.LLK1 - The log(likelihood that the ID from SNG.1ST is the correct assignment)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dksag","text":"9.     SNG.2ND - The next best singlet assignment for sample ID","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"eee9k","text":"10.   SNG.LLK2 - The log(likelihood that the ID from SNG.2ND is the correct assignment)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d6vrg","text":"11.   SNG.LLK0 - The log-likelihood from allele frequencies only","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b41v7","text":"12.   DBL.1ST - The sample ID that is most likely included if the assignment is a doublet","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"827f1","text":"13.   DBL.2ND - The sample ID that is next most likely included ifthe assignment is a doublet","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3l9ia","text":"14.   ALPHA - % Mixture Proportion","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8mvdc","text":"15.   LLK12 - The log(likelihood that the ID is a doublet)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4h77b","text":"16.   LLK1 - The log(likelihood that the ID from DBL.1ST is the correct singlet assignment)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ebmh8","text":"17.   LLK2 - The log(likelihood that the ID from DBL.2ND is the correct singlet assignment)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9kao2","text":"18.   LLK10 - The log(likelihood that the ID from DBL.1ST is one of the doublet, and the other doublet identity is calculated from allele frequencies only)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8i12j","text":"19.   LLK20 - The log(likelihood that the ID from DBL.2ND is one of the doublet, and the other doublet identity is calculated from allele frequencies only)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"419ge","text":"20.   LLK00 - The log(likelihood that the droplet is doublet, but both identities are calculated from allele frequencies only)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dvnca","text":"21.   PRB.DBL - Posterior probability of the doublet assignment","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1jk4v","text":"22.   PRB.SNG1 - Posterior probability of the singlet assignment when excluding all possible doublets","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9pddf","text":"
","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"9A72020C4C61416E9633E8FA15E25FE7","url":"https://github.com/statgen/demuxlet"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"6B6CBB48692C4C33A1C1BEE5F4F5F1A0","url":"mailto:jimmie.ye@ucsf.edu"}},"10":{"type":"code_insert","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":"$ brew install xz

steps: Please select between the two following options: 1. Installating from Docker 2. Installing from source

Running demuxlet from docker We have created a docker container at yimmieg/demuxlet to run demuxlet through docker. You can run it with $ docker run -v path/to/tutorial:/data yimmieg/demuxlet --sam /data/$bam --vcf /data/$vcf --field $(GT or GP or PL) --out /data/$filename Add bam file name for $bam and vcf file name for $vcf . Use <(zcat $vcf) or <(gzcat $vcf) if vcf file is compressed. The options for --field are individual genotypes (GT), posterior probability (GP), or genotype likelihood (PL). If using GT --field option for, you must include --geno-error , which is the genotype error rate.

demuxlet output The demuxlet software produces three output files. [prefix].best The .best file contains the assignments of the best sample identity (singlet: SNG- ; doublet: DBL- ; ambiguous: AMB-< > ) in the BEST column for each cell barcode identified in the BARCODE column along with details of the statistics used to determine the best identity. The [prefix].single file contains the statistics for matching each cell with each possible sample For complete descriptions of the columns in each output file, please see the demuxlet README.

Create directory and download datasets Now, let's first download the data we need. We are now providing a one-stop-shop to download all of the data you need for the tutorial: https://ucsf.box.com/s/vg1bycvsjgyg63gkqsputprq5rxzjl6k . After downloading and unzipping (it's BIG), you should have a directory called demuxlet.tutorial .

Run demuxlet $ cd demuxlet.tutorial $ docker run -v ./:/data yimmieg/demuxlet --sam /data/jurkat_293t_downsampled_n500_full_bam.bam --vcf /data/jurkat_293t_exons_only.vcf --field GT --out data/jurkat_293t_demuxlet $ cd demuxlet.tutorial $ docker run -v ./:/data yimmieg/demuxlet --sam /data/jurkat_293t_downsampled_n500_full_bam.bam --vcf /data/jurkat_293t_exons_only.vcf --field GT --out data/jurkat_293t_demuxlet

Compare the called genotypes vs transcriptome data In this analysis, we will use R to produce a t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) plot of the cells from the 293T:Jurkat 10x experiment with the cells colored by the assignments from the demuxlet pipeline. library(ggplot2); library(data.table); ## let's read in the barcodes tsne <- fread("analysis_csv/tsne/projection.csv"); demuxlet <- fread("jurkat_293t_demuxlet.best"); ## let's filter for the barcodes that we sampled df <- data.frame(tsne1=tsne$"TSNE-1"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], tsne2=tsne$"TSNE-2"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], doublet=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[1]]}), cell.type=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[2]]})) ggplot(aes(tsne1,tsne2,color=cell.type),data=df)+geom_point() library(ggplot2); library(data.table); ## let's read in the barcodes tsne <- fread("analysis_csv/tsne/projection.csv"); demuxlet <- fread("jurkat_293t_demuxlet.best"); ## let's filter for the barcodes that we sampled df <- data.frame(tsne1=tsne$"TSNE-1"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], tsne2=tsne$"TSNE-2"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], doublet=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[1]]}), cell.type=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[2]]})) ggplot(aes(tsne1,tsne2,color=cell.type),data=df)+geom_point() After this, you should get the following image:
Let's also take a look at the doublets. ggplot(aes(tsne1,tsne2,color=doublet),data=df)+geom_point() After that, you should get the following image:
Installing docker First, get docker for whatever platform you feel comfortable with: https://www.docker.com/get-started

Before installing demuxlet, you need to install htslib in the same directory you want to install demuxlet (i.e. demuxlet and htslib should be siblings). After installing htslib, you can clone the current snapshot of this repository to install as well $ git clone https://github.com/statgen/demuxlet.git $ cd demuxlet $ autoreconf -vfi $ ./configure (with additional options such as --prefix) $ make $ make install (may require root privilege) Remember to add demuxlet to $PATH

Create directory and download datasets Now, let's first download the data we need. We are now providing a one-stop-shop to download all of the data you need for the tutorial: https://ucsf.box.com/s/vg1bycvsjgyg63gkqsputprq5rxzjl6k . After downloading and unzipping (it's BIG), you should have a directory called demuxlet.tutorial .

Run demuxlet $ cd demuxlet.tutorial $ demuxlet --sam /data/jurkat_293t_downsampled_n500_full_bam.bam --vcf /data/jurkat_293t_exons_only.vcf --field GT --out data/jurkat_293t_demuxlet $ cd demuxlet.tutorial $ docker run -v ./:/data yimmieg/demuxlet --sam /data/jurkat_293t_downsampled_n500_full_bam.bam --vcf /data/jurkat_293t_exons_only.vcf --field GT --out data/jurkat_293t_demuxlet

Compare the called genotypes vs transcriptome data In this analysis, we will use R to produce a t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) plot of the cells from the 293T:Jurkat 10x experiment with the cells colored by the assignments from the demuxlet pipeline. library(ggplot2); library(data.table); ## let's read in the barcodes tsne <- fread("analysis_csv/tsne/projection.csv"); demuxlet <- fread("jurkat_293t_demuxlet.best"); ## let's filter for the barcodes that we sampled df <- data.frame(tsne1=tsne$"TSNE-1"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], tsne2=tsne$"TSNE-2"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], doublet=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[1]]}), cell.type=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[2]]})) ggplot(aes(tsne1,tsne2,color=cell.type),data=df)+geom_point() After this, you should get the following image:
Let's also take a look at the doublets. ggplot(aes(tsne1,tsne2,color=doublet),data=df)+geom_point() After that, you should get the following image:

Create directory and download datasets Now, let's first download the data we need. We are now providing a one-stop-shop to download all of the data you need for the tutorial: https://ucsf.box.com/s/vg1bycvsjgyg63gkqsputprq5rxzjl6k . After downloading and unzipping (it's BIG), you should have a directory called demuxlet.tutorial .

Run demuxlet $ cd demuxlet.tutorial $ docker run -v ./:/data yimmieg/demuxlet --sam /data/jurkat_293t_downsampled_n500_full_bam.bam --vcf /data/jurkat_293t_exons_only.vcf --field GT --out data/jurkat_293t_demuxlet $ cd demuxlet.tutorial $ docker run -v ./:/data yimmieg/demuxlet --sam /data/jurkat_293t_downsampled_n500_full_bam.bam --vcf /data/jurkat_293t_exons_only.vcf --field GT --out data/jurkat_293t_demuxlet

Compare the called genotypes vs transcriptome data In this analysis, we will use R to produce a t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) plot of the cells from the 293T:Jurkat 10x experiment with the cells colored by the assignments from the demuxlet pipeline. library(ggplot2); library(data.table); ## let's read in the barcodes tsne <- fread("analysis_csv/tsne/projection.csv"); demuxlet <- fread("jurkat_293t_demuxlet.best"); ## let's filter for the barcodes that we sampled df <- data.frame(tsne1=tsne$"TSNE-1"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], tsne2=tsne$"TSNE-2"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], doublet=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[1]]}), cell.type=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[2]]})) ggplot(aes(tsne1,tsne2,color=cell.type),data=df)+geom_point() library(ggplot2); library(data.table); ## let's read in the barcodes tsne <- fread("analysis_csv/tsne/projection.csv"); demuxlet <- fread("jurkat_293t_demuxlet.best"); ## let's filter for the barcodes that we sampled df <- data.frame(tsne1=tsne$"TSNE-1"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], tsne2=tsne$"TSNE-2"[na.omit(match(demuxlet$BARCODE,tsne$Barcode))], doublet=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[1]]}), cell.type=sapply(demuxlet$BEST,function(x){strsplit(x,"-")[[1]][[2]]})) ggplot(aes(tsne1,tsne2,color=cell.type),data=df)+geom_point() After this, you should get the following image:
Let's also take a look at the doublets. ggplot(aes(tsne1,tsne2,color=doublet),data=df)+geom_point() After that, you should get the following image:

Running demuxlet from docker We have created a docker container at yimmieg/demuxlet to run demuxlet through docker. You can run it with $ docker run -v path/to/tutorial:/data yimmieg/demuxlet --sam /data/$bam --vcf /data/$vcf --field $(GT or GP or PL) --out /data/$filename
